title: The Screening Clinic
subtitle: Why a 99%-accurate positive can still mean you're fine
realWorldHook: You run a screening clinic. A '99% accurate' test for a rare disease comes back positive, and a worried patient asks if they're going to die. Most people — including many doctors — get the honest answer badly wrong.
conceptSummary: With a rare condition, the few true positives are swamped by false positives drawn from the huge healthy group.

steps.title: Welcome to your clinic
steps.body:
Today you run a screening clinic. **1,000 people** line up for a test that's *'99% accurate'* for a disease only **1 in 1,000** people actually have.

Most will test negative. A small pile will test positive. Then one worried patient who came back positive will look you in the eye and ask: *am I going to die?*

The number you owe them is not the one almost everyone expects.

steps.prompt:
You tested positive on a 99%-accurate test for a disease **1 in 1,000** people have. What's the chance you *actually* have it?

The test [[catches 99% of real cases|This is the test's *sensitivity* — the share of truly sick people it correctly flags as positive. Even at 99%, it can't overcome a very rare disease.]] and only [[false-alarms 5% of the time|The *false-positive rate*: how often the test says 'positive' for someone who is actually healthy. A small rate across a huge healthy group still produces a flood of false alarms.]].
steps.revealMessage: Lock it in. Now run the clinic and just count the people.

revealByOption.a: That's the answer almost everyone gives — and it's the trap this clinic is about. The test really is accurate, yet the honest answer is far lower. Let's run 1,000 people and count.
revealByOption.b: Lower than most people guess — but still way too high. Run the clinic and count the real positives against the false ones.
revealByOption.c: You're onto it — and it's even more extreme than most believe. Run the clinic and watch the positive pile.

steps.prompt: Run your clinic. Drag the sliders for **how rare the disease is**, how good the test is at catching it, and how often it false-alarms — the grid and the live percentage update instantly. Watch the positive pile: a few *real* cases in rose, buried under the *false alarms* in amber.
Of everyone who tested positive, only about **9%** actually have the disease. How can a 99%-accurate test be right about your positive result less than 1 in 10 times?

options.label: The few true positives are swamped by false positives drawn from the huge healthy group
The test must be broken or miscalibrated
'99% accurate' means 99% of positives are correct

feedback.correct: Surprised? The test is accurate. Your *conclusion* still flips. With a rare disease, a handful of true positives gets buried under false positives pulled from the enormous healthy crowd. Count the people, not the percentages.
feedback.incorrect: The '99% accurate' is the test's reliability — not the chance you're sick given a positive. Think back to how the positive pile divided: most of it was healthy people the test false-flagged.

byOption.b: The test works exactly as advertised. The surprise comes from the [[base rate|How common the disease is *before* anyone is tested — here just 1 in 1,000. When it's this low, most positives are false no matter how good the test.]], not a faulty machine.
byOption.c: Accuracy describes how the test treats sick and healthy people — not your positive. With a rare disease, most positives still come from the healthy group the test [[false-flagged|A *false positive*: a healthy person the test wrongly marks as positive. Five percent of a thousand healthy people is fifty false alarms.]].

steps.hint: 5% of ~999 healthy people is about 50 false positives. How does that compare to the ~1 true positive?

steps.title: Count people, not percentages
steps.body:
Percentages hide the trap; **natural frequencies** expose it. Don't say '99% accurate' — say it in *people*:

Out of 1,000, about **1** person is sick and tests positive. Of the other 999 healthy people, about **50** also test positive. So ~51 people hold a positive slip, and only **1** is truly sick.

That's roughly 1 in 51 — about **2%**. The machine never lied. The crowd is just overwhelmingly healthy.

steps.prompt: Here's the same count written as a formula. Reading it, what does the chance a positive is real actually equal?

options.label: True positives divided by all positives (true + false)
True positives divided by the whole population of 1,000

feedback.correct: Exactly — and the formula only makes sense once the intuition has landed. The chance a positive is real is the rose count over the rose-plus-amber count: true positives over *all* positives.
feedback.incorrect: Look at the fraction: the top is the real cases, and the bottom is *every* positive slip — real and false alarm alike.

byOption.b: That's the input, not the answer. 99% is how the test treats people; the formula divides the real positives by the whole positive pile to get your chance.
byOption.c: The denominator isn't everyone — it's everyone who *tested positive*. The healthy negatives never enter this fraction.

steps.hint: Cover the negatives entirely. Of just the positive slips, what share are the real (rose) ones?

steps.title: You now read 'positive' correctly
steps.body:
This same trap reaches far beyond your clinic. It convicts the innocent when a courtroom hears '1-in-a-million DNA match' and forgets how many innocent people that still implicates ([[the prosecutor's fallacy|Confusing how rare the evidence is with how likely the suspect is guilty — exactly the base-rate slip you just untangled. A rare match across millions of people still points at several innocents.]]). It flags innocent travelers when a rare threat meets a huge crowd.

Whenever someone hands you a '99% accurate' result, your first question is now reflexive: *how rare is the thing it's testing for?* Count the people, and the honest answer appears.
